metadata.description: IOPrescriber on 50 randomly sampled production Polaris logs

parsed.diagnosis: This HPC job exhibits severe small I/O access patterns that are causing significant performance degradation. With 92,955 POSIX reads and 4,069 POSIX writes, the job is performing 97,024 total I/O operations but only achieving 399.5 MB/s bandwidth. The small_io_ratio of 0.7393 indicates that 74% of I/O operations are small transfers. The average read size of 146KB and write size of 266KB are both well below the 1MB threshold for efficient I/O. Additionally, 70,350 unaligned file accesses are causing further overhead. The high operation count combined with small transfer sizes creates excessive syscall overhead that dominates the I/O time.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs 97,024 I/O operations with average sizes of 146KB (reads) and 266KB (writes), both well below the 1MB efficiency threshold. This creates excessive syscall overhead as each small operation requires a separate system call.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-4x based on reducing syscall overhead

recommendations.explanation: With has_mpiio=1.0 indicating MPI-IO availability and collective_ratio=0.3897 showing underutilized collective operations, switching from individual POSIX calls to MPI-IO collective operations with larger transfer sizes can significantly improve performance.
recommendations.code_before: write(fd, buf, nbytes)  /* GenericIO.cxx, POSIX shared file */
recommendations.code_after: MPI_File_write_at_all(fh, offset, buf, count, MPI_FLOAT, &status)  /* GenericIO.cxx */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-4x from collective I/O and larger datatypes

recommendations.explanation: The 70,350 unaligned file accesses (POSIX_FILE_NOT_ALIGNED) are causing additional overhead. Aligning I/O operations to filesystem block boundaries can reduce this overhead.
recommendations.code_before:
/* Unaligned access pattern */
write(fd, buf, size);
recommendations.expected_speedup: 1.2-1.5x from reduced alignment overhead

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 3-6x total speedup from combining buffering, collective I/O, and alignment optimizations

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits severe I/O inefficiency due to excessive synchronization patterns. With fsync_ratio=4.0, the application is calling fsync 4 times more frequently than necessary, creating a major bottleneck. The job writes only 267 bytes total with seq_write_ratio=1.0 and small_write_ratio=1.0, indicating very small sequential writes that are being over-synchronized. Despite reading 1.85GB efficiently (avg_read_size=462MB), the write pattern is destroying overall throughput utilization.

recommendations.explanation: The fsync_ratio=4.0 indicates excessive synchronization - the application is calling fsync after every small write operation instead of batching writes and syncing once at the end. With only 267 bytes written total, this creates massive overhead.
recommendations.expected_speedup: Significant improvement in write throughput based on benchmark evidence showing this pattern as primary cause of throughput_utilization bottleneck
recommendations.api_change: Remove per-write fsync calls, retain single final fsync

recommendations.explanation: The small write pattern (avg_write_size=267 bytes, small_write_ratio=1.0) combined with sequential access suggests the application could benefit from write aggregation to reduce system call overhead and improve bandwidth utilization.
recommendations.expected_speedup: Moderate improvement by reducing system call overhead for small I/O operations
recommendations.api_change: Buffer aggregation before write system calls

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 2-5x improvement in write performance based on benchmark evidence showing fsync elimination as primary optimization for throughput_utilization bottlenecks

parsed.diagnosis: This HPC job exhibits severe I/O inefficiency due to excessive small write operations. With 1,533 POSIX writes totaling 1.26GB, the average write size is only ~821KB, but critically, 1,027 writes are in the 0-100 byte range (POSIX_SIZE_WRITE_0_100=1027). This creates massive syscall overhead as the application performs many tiny writes instead of aggregating data into larger transfers. The 1,109 unaligned file accesses (POSIX_FILE_NOT_ALIGNED) further compound the problem by forcing the filesystem to perform read-modify-write cycles.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs 1,027 writes of 0-100 bytes each, causing excessive syscall overhead. Small writes should be buffered and flushed in large chunks (>=1MB) to minimize system call frequency and improve bandwidth utilization.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-3x based on benchmark evidence showing dramatic improvement when moving from small to large transfer sizes

recommendations.explanation: For applications using MPI, collective I/O with larger datatypes can eliminate the small write problem entirely by coordinating writes across processes and using optimized MPI-IO implementations.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* Individual small writes */
recommendations.code_after: MPI_File_write_at_all(fh, offset, buf, count, MPI_FLOAT, &status)  /* Collective I/O with larger transfers */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-3x based on benchmark evidence from HACC-IO proxy showing collective I/O performance gains

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 2-4x total I/O performance improvement by eliminating small write overhead and reducing syscall frequency from 1,533 to ~100-200 large writes

parsed.diagnosis: This HPC job exhibits two critical I/O performance problems: (1) Small transfer granularity - with 5,597 unaligned file accesses and high syscall counts (1,252 ACCESS1 operations), the application is making many small I/O requests instead of batching them into larger transfers. The small_io_ratio of 0.5392 confirms that over half the I/O operations are inefficiently small. (2) Non-sequential access patterns - despite having reasonable sequential ratios (71% writes, 78% reads), the 5,438 total writes with unaligned access suggest fragmented I/O that defeats storage prefetching. The high metadata_time_ratio of 0.6187 indicates excessive time spent on file operations rather than data transfer.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs 5,438 writes and 3,800 reads with many small transfers (avg_write_size=103KB, small_io_ratio=0.5392). This creates excessive syscall overhead as shown by 1,252 ACCESS1_COUNT operations. The benchmark evidence shows that buffering small writes into >=1MB chunks eliminates this overhead.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-3x based on transfer size optimization

recommendations.explanation: With 5,597 unaligned file accesses and seq_write_ratio of only 0.7111, the application has fragmented access patterns that prevent effective storage prefetching. The 406 ACCESS4_COUNT operations suggest scattered I/O requests that could benefit from collective MPI-IO operations.
recommendations.code_before: write(fd, buf, nbytes)  /* POSIX shared file */
recommendations.code_after: MPI_File_write_at_all(fh, offset, buf, count, MPI_FLOAT, &status)  /* GenericIO.cxx */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 1.5-2x from collective I/O coordination

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 3-5x total speedup from combining transfer size optimization with collective I/O patterns

parsed.diagnosis: This HPC job exhibits severe I/O inefficiency due to excessive small-granularity operations. With 5,370 POSIX access operations, 2,901 writes, and 8,850 reads across 64 processes, the job is performing many small I/O calls instead of fewer large ones. The small_io_ratio of 0.6927 indicates that 69% of operations are small transfers. Despite having MPI-IO available (has_mpiio=1.0), the job shows 3,551 unaligned file accesses, suggesting suboptimal I/O patterns that cause excessive syscall overhead and poor bandwidth utilization.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs excessive small I/O operations (5,370 POSIX accesses, avg_write_size=197KB, avg_read_size=1.2MB) instead of aggregating into large transfers. Small writes cause syscall overhead and prevent efficient storage system utilization.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-4x based on HACC-IO benchmarks showing dramatic improvement when moving from small to 1MB+ transfers
recommendations.api_change: POSIX write buffering -> aggregated large writes

recommendations.explanation: With MPI-IO available (has_mpiio=1.0) and collective_ratio=0.5046, the job should leverage collective I/O operations with larger datatypes instead of individual small operations to reduce coordination overhead.
recommendations.code_before: MPI_File_write_at(fh, offset, buf, count, MPI_FLOAT, &status)  /* individual small writes */
recommendations.code_after: MPI_File_write_at_all(fh, offset, buf, count, MPI_FLOAT, &status)  /* collective I/O with larger datatypes */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 1.5-2x based on HACC-IO collective I/O patterns showing improved coordination and reduced overhead

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 3-6x total speedup by combining write buffering (2-4x) with collective I/O optimization (1.5-2x)

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits severe I/O performance problems with 16,128 small write operations (100K-1MB range) across 256 processes writing to only 9 files. The primary issues are: (1) Excessive syscall overhead from small transfer sizes despite 1MB average writes, indicating many sub-optimal operations mixed with larger ones, and (2) File strategy problems with 256 processes contending for just 9 shared files, creating serialization bottlenecks. The 719.9 seconds of total write time with only 23.5 MB/s bandwidth indicates severe inefficiency.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs 16,128 write operations with many in the 100K-1MB range, causing excessive syscall overhead. Despite an average write size of 1MB, the high operation count suggests many smaller writes are mixed in, fragmenting I/O efficiency.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, small_size);  /* Multiple small writes */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-3x based on reducing syscall overhead

recommendations.explanation: 256 processes writing to only 9 files creates severe contention and serialization. With 719.9 seconds of write time spread across processes, shared file access is creating bottlenecks that limit scalability.
recommendations.code_before: write(fd, buf, nbytes)  /* POSIX shared file access */
recommendations.code_after: MPI_File_write_at_all(fh, offset, buf, count, MPI_FLOAT, &status)  /* Collective MPI-IO */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 1.5-2x based on collective I/O reducing contention

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 3-6x total speedup (multiplicative effect of both optimizations)

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits severe access granularity problems with 700,402 POSIX read operations averaging only 8.2KB per call. With 5.7GB total data read through such small operations, the job suffers from excessive syscall overhead. The high number of unaligned accesses (700,401) and consecutive reads (700,401) indicates a pattern of many tiny, sequential read operations that could be aggregated into much larger transfers.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs 700,402 POSIX reads averaging 8.2KB each, causing massive syscall overhead. Small reads should be aggregated into 1MB+ buffers to reduce system call frequency and improve bandwidth utilization.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 16x based on transfer size increase from 8KB to 1MB
recommendations.api_change: POSIX read pattern optimization (same API, different usage)

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 10-20x speedup based on eliminating 99% of syscall overhead through read aggregation

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits severe I/O inefficiency due to extremely small write operations. With 195,889 POSIX writes totaling only 5.4MB of data, the average write size is just 27.5 bytes. This creates massive syscall overhead - the job is making nearly 200,000 system calls for what should be accomplished with a handful of large writes. The 99.84% small I/O ratio confirms that virtually all operations are sub-optimal. Additionally, 195,832 unaligned accesses further degrade performance by forcing the storage system to perform read-modify-write cycles.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs 195,889 tiny writes averaging 27.5 bytes each, causing excessive syscall overhead. Each write() call has significant kernel overhead regardless of size, so making 200K calls instead of ~6 calls (for 5.4MB total) wastes enormous CPU cycles and I/O bandwidth.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 27);  /* 27B per call, 195K calls */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 10-50x based on reducing 195K syscalls to ~6 syscalls

recommendations.explanation: The 195,832 unaligned accesses force storage systems to perform read-modify-write operations, significantly degrading performance. Aligning writes to filesystem block boundaries (typically 4KB) eliminates this overhead.
recommendations.code_before: write(fd, buf, size);  /* arbitrary offset, causes unaligned access */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-5x for unaligned access elimination

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 20-100x total speedup by combining buffering (10-50x) and alignment (2-5x) optimizations

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits severe throughput underutilization due to excessive synchronization patterns. The key evidence: fsync_ratio=4.0 indicates 4x more fsync calls than writes, small_write_ratio=1.0 shows all writes are small (avg 306 bytes), and seq_write_ratio=1.0 with only 306 total bytes written suggests frequent tiny sequential writes. The job performs 12 file operations with minimal write volume but disproportionate synchronization overhead, creating a classic small-write + excessive-fsync bottleneck that severely limits I/O throughput.

recommendations.explanation: The fsync_ratio=4.0 means the application calls fsync 4 times for every write operation, creating massive synchronization overhead. With only 306 bytes written total across 12 files, this suggests fsync after every tiny write operation, which forces expensive disk flushes.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 4x reduction in sync overhead based on fsync_ratio improvement

recommendations.explanation: small_write_ratio=1.0 and avg_write_size=306 bytes indicates all writes are extremely small. This creates high per-operation overhead and poor bandwidth utilization. Buffering small writes into larger operations would improve efficiency.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-3x improvement from reduced syscall overhead

recommendations.explanation: With 12 files and minimal write volume, the application may be creating unnecessary file fragmentation. Consolidating writes or using fewer files could reduce metadata overhead and improve sequential access patterns.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 1.5-2x from reduced file metadata operations

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 6-12x total speedup (4x from fsync elimination + 2-3x from write buffering)

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits severe throughput underutilization due to excessive synchronization patterns. The key evidence: fsync_ratio=4.0 indicates 4x more fsync calls than necessary, small_write_ratio=1.0 shows all writes are small (avg_write_size=253 bytes), and seq_write_ratio=1.0 with only 253 total bytes written suggests many tiny sequential writes. The job performs 12 file operations with minimal data (253 bytes written vs 3.6MB read), creating a metadata-heavy workload that serializes on storage servers.

recommendations.explanation: The fsync_ratio=4.0 indicates excessive synchronization - likely calling fsync after every small write operation (253 bytes average). This forces immediate disk commits and prevents write batching, severely limiting throughput.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 4x based on fsync_ratio reduction from 4.0 to 1.0

recommendations.explanation: Small write operations (253 bytes average, small_write_ratio=1.0) create excessive system call overhead. Buffering these into larger writes would reduce per-operation costs and improve bandwidth utilization.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-3x based on reduced system call overhead for small I/O

recommendations.explanation: With 12 files and minimal write data (253 bytes total), this appears to be a metadata-heavy workload that may benefit from file consolidation to reduce open/close overhead and improve Lustre striping efficiency.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 1.5-2x based on reduced file operation overhead

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 6-12x speedup (4x from fsync reduction × 2-3x from write buffering)

parsed.diagnosis: This HPC job exhibits severe access granularity problems with 15,808 small POSIX read operations and poor alignment (15,899 unaligned accesses). The average read size of 8,183 bytes is far below the optimal 1MB threshold, causing excessive syscall overhead. With 15,786 individual access operations and high consecutive read patterns (15,799), the job is performing many small, sequential reads instead of fewer large transfers. The 972 files and 23% metadata time ratio indicate fragmented I/O patterns that compound the small transfer problem.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs 15,808 small POSIX reads averaging 8,183 bytes each, causing excessive syscall overhead. Small transfers (<1MB) prevent efficient I/O pipeline utilization and increase per-operation latency.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 10-15x based on benchmark evidence showing dramatic improvement when moving from small to 1MB+ transfers
recommendations.api_change: POSIX read pattern optimization - aggregate small reads into large buffers

recommendations.explanation: With MPI-IO available (has_mpiio=1.0) and collective_ratio=1.0, the job should leverage collective I/O operations instead of individual POSIX calls to reduce overhead and improve bandwidth utilization.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 5-10x based on collective I/O benchmarks showing significant improvement over independent access patterns
recommendations.api_change: POSIX read -> MPI_File_read_all with collective operations

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 15-25x total speedup by combining large transfer sizes with collective I/O operations

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits severe access granularity problems with 2,687 POSIX write operations and 2,711 unaligned file accesses. The average write size of 32.7KB is far below the optimal 1MB threshold, causing excessive syscall overhead. With 2,662 small access operations and nearly all writes being sequential (99.8%), this is a classic case of inefficient small I/O patterns that can be easily optimized through write buffering.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs 2,687 small POSIX writes averaging 32.7KB each, well below the 1MB optimal threshold. This creates excessive syscall overhead as each write triggers a separate kernel call.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 32768);  /* 32KB per call */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-3x based on reducing syscall overhead

recommendations.explanation: The 2,711 unaligned file accesses compound the small I/O problem by forcing additional filesystem overhead. Combined with small transfer sizes, this creates a multiplicative performance penalty.
recommendations.code_before: write(fd, buf, size);  /* Unaligned, small transfers */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 1.5-2x from eliminating alignment penalties

recommendations.explanation: Consider switching from individual POSIX writes to MPI-IO collective operations to leverage parallel filesystem optimizations, especially given the sequential write pattern (99.8%).
recommendations.code_before: write(fd, buf, size);  /* Individual POSIX writes */
recommendations.code_after: MPI_File_write_at_all(fh, offset, buf, count, MPI_BYTE, &status);  /* Collective MPI-IO */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-4x from collective I/O optimizations

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 4-8x total speedup from combining write buffering, alignment, and potentially collective I/O

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits severe throughput underutilization due to excessive synchronization patterns. The key indicators are: (1) fsync_ratio=6.0, meaning 6 fsync calls per write operation - this is extremely high and forces immediate disk commits; (2) small_write_ratio=1.0 with avg_write_size=488 bytes, indicating all writes are small and inefficient; (3) seq_write_ratio=1.0 shows sequential access but the combination with excessive fsync calls serializes I/O operations. The job wrote only 488 bytes total but performed multiple fsync operations, creating a pathological sync-after-every-write pattern that destroys throughput.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs fsync after every write operation (fsync_ratio=6.0), which forces immediate disk synchronization and eliminates any buffering benefits. With only 488 bytes written total, this creates massive overhead.
recommendations.expected_speedup: Significant improvement based on removing per-write synchronization overhead
recommendations.api_change: POSIX write pattern optimization - reduce fsync frequency

recommendations.explanation: All writes are small (avg_write_size=488 bytes, small_write_ratio=1.0), creating high per-operation overhead. Buffering multiple small writes into larger operations would improve efficiency.
recommendations.expected_speedup: Moderate improvement from reduced system call overhead

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 2-5x speedup range based on eliminating excessive fsync overhead and improving write batching efficiency

parsed.diagnosis: This HPC job exhibits severe I/O inefficiency due to excessive small-granularity access patterns. With 2,809 POSIX access operations and 5,006 unaligned file accesses across 6,674 reads and 1,113 writes, the job is performing many small I/O operations instead of fewer large ones. The small_io_ratio of 0.7468 indicates that 74.68% of I/O operations are small transfers, causing significant syscall overhead. Despite having MPI-IO available (has_mpiio=1.0), the job appears to be using inefficient POSIX I/O patterns for shared file access.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs 2,809 small POSIX access operations with high unaligned access count (5,006), causing excessive syscall overhead. The benchmark evidence shows that small transfer sizes (<1MB) create significant performance bottlenecks that can be resolved by buffering small writes into large chunks.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-4x based on HACC-IO benchmark evidence showing dramatic improvement when moving from small to large transfer sizes

recommendations.explanation: Since the job has MPI-IO available (has_mpiio=1.0) but shows POSIX-dominated access patterns, migrating to collective MPI-IO operations would eliminate the small access overhead while providing better parallel I/O performance for the 64 processes.
recommendations.code_before: write(fd, buf, nbytes)  /* GenericIO.cxx, POSIX shared file */
recommendations.code_after: MPI_File_write_at_all(fh, offset, buf, count, MPI_FLOAT, &status)  /* GenericIO.cxx */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 1.5-3x based on benchmark evidence showing collective I/O performance improvements over individual POSIX calls

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 3-6x total speedup by combining buffered large transfers with collective MPI-IO operations

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits severe throughput underutilization due to excessive synchronization patterns. The key evidence: fsync_ratio=4.0 indicates 4x more fsync calls than writes, seq_write_ratio=1.0 with small_write_ratio=1.0 shows sequential but tiny writes (avg 262 bytes), and POSIX_BYTES_WRITTEN=262 total suggests a single small write followed by multiple fsync calls. This creates a pathological pattern where the application spends more time in synchronization overhead than actual I/O, severely limiting throughput despite good sequential access patterns.

recommendations.explanation: The fsync_ratio=4.0 indicates excessive synchronization - 4 fsync calls for every write operation. With only 262 bytes written total, this suggests fsync is being called after every small write, creating massive overhead. The benchmark evidence shows this pattern destroys throughput.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 4x reduction in sync overhead based on fsync_ratio improvement
recommendations.api_change: POSIX write pattern optimization - reduce fsync frequency

recommendations.explanation: The combination of small_write_ratio=1.0 and avg_write_size=262 bytes indicates all writes are extremely small. This creates high per-operation overhead and poor bandwidth utilization. Buffering writes into larger chunks will improve throughput efficiency.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-3x improvement from reduced per-operation overhead

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 5-10x total speedup from eliminating sync overhead and improving write efficiency

parsed.diagnosis: This HPC job suffers from severe access granularity issues with extremely small I/O operations. The Darshan data shows an average write size of only 136.5 bytes and average read size of 4.9KB, both far below the 1MB threshold for efficient I/O. With 23,160 total I/O operations (9,285 writes + 13,875 reads) and 20,761 unaligned accesses, the job is experiencing massive syscall overhead. The small_io_ratio of 0.63 confirms that 63% of operations are inefficiently small. At only 6.2 MB/s total bandwidth across 72 processes, this job is severely I/O bound due to excessive fine-grained access patterns.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs 9,285 write operations with an average size of only 136.5 bytes, causing excessive syscall overhead. Each small write triggers a separate system call, creating massive overhead when multiplied across thousands of operations.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 136);  /* 136B per call */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 10-50x based on KB evidence from similar small write patterns
recommendations.api_change: POSIX write buffering -> large chunk writes

recommendations.explanation: The job performs 13,875 read operations with an average size of 4.9KB, still well below the 1MB efficiency threshold. These small reads create unnecessary syscall overhead and prevent efficient disk/network utilization.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) read(fd, buf, 4872);  /* 4.9KB per call */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 5-20x based on KB evidence from aggregated read patterns
recommendations.api_change: POSIX small reads -> large buffered reads

recommendations.explanation: With 20,761 unaligned accesses and MPI present (has_apmpi=1), the job could benefit from MPI-IO collective operations to coordinate I/O across the 72 processes and achieve better alignment and larger transfer sizes.
recommendations.code_before:
/* Individual process POSIX I/O */
write(fd, local_buf, small_size);
recommendations.code_after:
/* MPI-IO collective with larger datatypes */
MPI_File_write_all(fh, collective_buf, large_count, MPI_BYTE, &status);
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-5x based on collective I/O coordination benefits

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 20-100x total speedup range based on eliminating small I/O overhead across all operations

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits severe throughput underutilization due to excessive synchronization patterns. The key indicators are: (1) fsync_ratio=4.0, meaning 4x more fsync calls than necessary, (2) small_write_ratio=1.0 with only 357 bytes written total, indicating tiny writes that trigger frequent synchronization, (3) seq_write_ratio=1.0 showing sequential access that should be efficient but isn't due to sync overhead. The job performs 58MB of reads efficiently (avg_read_size=14.6MB) but writes are pathologically small and over-synchronized.

recommendations.explanation: The fsync_ratio=4.0 indicates excessive synchronization after every small write operation. With only 357 bytes total written but 4x fsync calls, the job is calling fsync after every tiny write, causing severe performance degradation due to forced disk flushes.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 4x reduction in sync overhead based on fsync_ratio improvement

recommendations.explanation: The small_write_ratio=1.0 with avg_write_size=357 bytes indicates all writes are extremely small. This creates inefficient I/O patterns that don't utilize available bandwidth. The job should buffer writes to achieve larger, more efficient I/O operations.
recommendations.code_before:
/* Multiple tiny writes */
write(fd, small_buf, 357);  /* repeated small writes */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-3x improvement from reduced syscall overhead

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 4-8x speedup range based on eliminating excessive fsync calls (4x) and improving write efficiency (2x), with potential for multiplicative benefits

parsed.diagnosis: This HPC job exhibits severe I/O inefficiencies with 1,994 POSIX write operations averaging only 91KB per write, well below the 1MB threshold for efficient I/O. The job shows poor sequential access patterns (22% sequential write ratio, 23% sequential read ratio) across 3,029 files, causing excessive syscall overhead and defeating storage prefetching mechanisms. With 948 writes in the 1K-10K range and 175 writes in the 100K-1M range, the application is performing many small, fragmented I/O operations that severely limit bandwidth utilization.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs 1,994 POSIX writes with an average size of only 91KB, causing excessive syscall overhead. Small writes (1K-10K: 948 operations, 100K-1M: 175 operations) dominate the I/O pattern, preventing efficient bandwidth utilization.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-4x based on reducing syscall overhead

recommendations.explanation: Poor sequential access ratios (22% write, 23% read) across 3,029 files indicate random access patterns that defeat OS read-ahead and storage prefetching. The high fsync ratio (1.9985) suggests frequent synchronization that fragments I/O streams.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 1.5-2x based on improved prefetching and reduced sync overhead
recommendations.api_change: Access pattern reorganization with batched synchronization

recommendations.explanation: With collective_ratio=1.0 indicating MPI-IO availability but poor performance characteristics, switching to MPI-IO collective operations could leverage coordinated I/O across the 8 processes for better bandwidth utilization.
recommendations.code_before: write(fd, buf, nbytes)  /* POSIX shared file */
recommendations.code_after: MPI_File_write_at_all(fh, offset, buf, count, MPI_FLOAT, &status)  /* MPI-IO collective */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 1.2-1.8x based on collective I/O coordination

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 3-8x total speedup by combining buffering (2-4x), sequential access (1.5-2x), and collective I/O (1.2-1.8x)

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits severe I/O inefficiency due to excessive synchronization patterns. With fsync_ratio=4.0, the application is calling fsync 4 times more frequently than necessary, forcing immediate disk commits after small writes (avg_write_size=321 bytes). The combination of small writes (321 bytes) with frequent synchronization creates a pathological pattern where the storage system cannot achieve good throughput. Despite reasonable overall bandwidth (515 MB/s), the write pattern is highly inefficient with only 321 bytes written total across 12 files, suggesting many tiny synchronized writes.

recommendations.explanation: The application is calling fsync after every small write operation (fsync_ratio=4.0 with avg_write_size=321 bytes). This forces the storage system to commit data to disk immediately, preventing write coalescing and dramatically reducing throughput. The benchmark evidence shows this is the primary cause of throughput_utilization bottlenecks.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 4x reduction in sync overhead based on fsync_ratio=4.0
recommendations.api_change: POSIX write pattern optimization - reduce fsync frequency

parsed.diagnosis: This HPC job exhibits severe small I/O access patterns that are causing significant performance degradation. With 102,805 POSIX reads and 9,547 POSIX writes, the job is performing excessive system calls with small transfer sizes. The average read size is only 137KB and write size is 295KB, both well below the 1MB threshold for efficient I/O. The 73,711 unaligned file accesses further compound the problem by forcing additional overhead. With 64.39% of I/O operations being small transfers and high syscall counts (34,367 access operations), the job is spending excessive time in kernel transitions rather than actual data movement.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs 102,805 small reads (avg 137KB) and 9,547 small writes (avg 295KB), causing excessive syscall overhead. Small I/O operations dominate 64.39% of all transfers, leading to poor bandwidth utilization.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-5x based on benchmark evidence showing dramatic improvement when moving from small to large transfer sizes
recommendations.api_change: POSIX write buffering -> large chunk writes

recommendations.explanation: With MPI-IO available (has_mpiio=1.0) and collective_ratio=0.4133, the job should leverage collective I/O operations with larger datatypes instead of individual small POSIX calls to reduce the 34,367 access operations.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
recommendations.code_after:
/* Use MPI-IO collective operations with larger transfers */
MPI_File_write_at_all(fh, offset, buf, count, MPI_FLOAT, &status);  /* >=1MB chunks */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-4x based on collective I/O reducing syscall overhead and improving coordination

recommendations.explanation: The 73,711 unaligned file accesses compound the small I/O problem by adding alignment overhead. Combining alignment fixes with larger transfer sizes will provide additional performance gains.
recommendations.code_before: write(fd, buf, nbytes)  /* unaligned, small transfers */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 1.5-2x additional improvement when combined with large transfers
recommendations.api_change: unaligned POSIX write -> aligned buffered write

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 5-10x total speedup by eliminating small I/O overhead and leveraging collective operations

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits severe throughput underutilization due to excessive synchronization patterns. The key indicators are: (1) fsync_ratio=4.0, meaning 4x more fsync calls than necessary, (2) small_write_ratio=1.0 with only 239 bytes written total, indicating tiny writes that trigger frequent synchronization, (3) seq_write_ratio=1.0 showing sequential access but poor batching. The job performs 468MB of reads but only 239 bytes of writes across 12 files, suggesting a read-heavy workload with inefficient checkpoint/metadata writes that are being over-synchronized.

recommendations.explanation: The fsync_ratio=4.0 indicates excessive synchronization after every small write operation. With only 239 bytes written total but 4x fsync calls, the job is calling fsync after every tiny write instead of batching writes and syncing once at the end.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 4x reduction in sync overhead based on fsync_ratio improvement

recommendations.explanation: The small_write_ratio=1.0 with avg_write_size=239 bytes indicates extremely small writes that create overhead. These should be buffered and written in larger chunks to improve throughput efficiency.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-3x improvement in write throughput by reducing syscall overhead
